architecture.summary: A sequence-to-sequence approach for cross-lingual aspect-based sentiment analysis that fine-tunes multilingual encoder-decoder models (mT5, mBART) on source-language data and applies them to target languages. Element markers ([A] for aspect, [C] for category, [P] for polarity) guide output generation. Constrained decoding dynamically restricts token candidates at each generation step based on the current state and schema information, preventing the model from generating aspect terms in the source language instead of the target language.

biblio.title: Advancing Cross-lingual Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis with LLMs and Constrained Decoding for Sequence-to-Sequence Models

claims.dataset: SemEval-2016 (E2E-ABSA, ACTE, TASD across 5 target languages: Spanish, French, Dutch, Russian, Turkish)
claims.statement: Constrained decoding improves cross-lingual ABSA performance by up to 10%
mT5 with constrained decoding achieves F1 of 69.3±1.0 on E2E-ABSA Spanish cross-lingual zero-shot
mT5 with constrained decoding outperforms GPT-4o mini by approximately 20% on the TASD task across all languages in zero-shot cross-lingual settings
LLaMA 3.1 fine-tuned achieves F1 of 77.4±0.5 on E2E-ABSA Spanish monolingual
mT5 requires 4.40 times less training time per epoch and 4.33 times less inference time per example than LLaMA 3.1
Fine-tuned multilingual LLaMA 3.1 achieves comparable results to mT5 with constrained decoding in cross-lingual ABSA, while English-centric LLaMA 3 struggles with cross-lingual tasks
GPT-4o mini shows notably lower performance on ACTE and TASD tasks involving aspect category identification, with performance gaps of 10–20% even with few-shot prompts
Results for Turkish are consistently worse than for other languages, likely because it is the only language in the evaluation not from the Indo-European family
